Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 107 actions; a strong score needs about 55 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Only 1 meaningful change found - and that was just the 1-pixel timer change from SPACE. So none of my 107 actions have actually changed the gameplay objects meaningfully. This is very concerning.
Let me re-examine the game. I've been using UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE but none of them have changed the actual game objects. The only change was a 1-pixel timer change.
Maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on objects. Let me look at the current board structure more carefully and try clicking on the center objects.
Looking at the images:
- Top-left panel: white rectangle on top, purple rectangle below (this seems like a "target" or "goal" display)
- Top-right: two small boxes with charcoal borders, one has white inside, one has purple inside
- Center: a gray-bordered purple rectangle, and below it a purple/white stacked rectangle
Wait, looking at step 104 image, there's a diamond shape in the center. But in steps 105-107, the center shows a gray-bordered purple rectangle. This means something DID change between step 104 and 105. But my analysis says there were no meaningful changes...
Let me re-examine. The issue might be that I'm looking at transitions but missing earlier ones. Let me check the history more carefully.
Actually, looking at the images again:
- Step 104: shows a diamond shape (purple with gray border) and a purple/white rectangle below
- Step 105-107: shows a gray-bordered purple rectangle and a purple/white rectangle below
So something changed between 104 and 105. But my transition analysis only found 1 meaningful change. This is strange. Maybe the change happened before I started tracking, or maybe the diamond shape is actually the same as the gray-bordered rectangle but rotated differently.
Wait, let me re-read the segmentation data. Node 11 is gray (g) with 30 pixels - that's the border. Node 12 is purple (p) with 84 pixels - that's the content inside. The boundary of node 11 is [[24,25], [24,38], [32,38], [25,38]] and node 12 is [[25,26], [25,37], [31,37], [31,26]]. So the border is at rows 24-32, cols 25-38 and the content is at rows 25-31, cols 26-37.
The diamond in step 104 might be a different shape. Let me check if the hash of node 12 changed between frames.
Actually, I think the key insight is that I need to use MOUSE. Let me try clicking on the center objects to see if that changes anything.
Let me try clicking on the purple rectangle in the center (node 12) to see what happens.

sections.content: Only 1-pixel timer changes found across all 107 actions - none of my directional/SPACE actions have changed gameplay objects. I need to try MOUSE actions. Let me click on the center objects:

# Let me try clicking on it

action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 28, 'col': 31}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
